metadata.title:
How Organization of Bust
ness Men in Los Angeles
Has Descended to Perse-
cution Of Messenger Boys
in Fight to Establish
"Open Shop"

pages.raw_text:
LOS ANGELES, CAL, Dec.
3.-The Merchants and
Manufacturers association,
representing "big business"
in it's fight here to break the
man who works, has started
a war on children in. the fur
therance of it's scheme to
down anything resembling
organized labor.


The children are the messenger
boys employed by the Western
Union telegraph Co. The strike is
story of petty arrests by the po-
lice of these kids" who work for
$50 to $50 month. 1t is a pitiful
story of how a grasping corpora
ton, assisted by an organization of
business men is trying to break
the kids" because they cought JUS,
tice only. 1t is another chapter in
the struggle between big business
and labor in Los Angeles, and be-
hind the scenes, helping the tele
graph company, is the M. & NJ,
whose members ranks as 1.-The best
citizens" of Los Angeles.


But listen to Frank Eckstrom
one of the boy strikers, he tells the
story of the troubles between the
messenger boys and the telegraph
trust" big business, the M. & M
and the Los Angeles police force,
all the interests allied to break a
few kids because they have organ.
lied for protection.


you see," he began, "I worked
for 'em first about five years ago
l was little and they could bull.
doze me. We only got a cent and a
half for message then. The com
pany has always kept too many
boys, SOS not to lose any messages,
but when there's so many of us we
can't make morn'n S. a week. They
wouldn't let us go home if we was
sick, and if we failed to show UP
Sunday we got locked 81, even II
we hadn't made anything that
week. When we was late they
fined us 20 cents an hour.


I'VE had to buy our own wheels
and pay for keeping them in order
and we are always in danger OF
bein' killed. If boy got in a col.
Iision and was hurt the company
didn't care any more than If he was
yellow dog. Amongst the kids
when a fellow was hurt and down
we always rustled around and took
up collections and tried to help
but nix from the company.


Then there was always favorit
ism. Some few Of the kids got all
the gravy"


."Gravy?" -,


81 mean the good assignments,
hotels and things like that, where
you deliver a big bunch of mes
sages at once and make good
thing. The rest of the kids had
to take out-of-the-way places, where
we rode long distance and got
cents.'


Don't you get 10 cents for a
message?"


mot SOS you could notice It.
The company always writes on the
message 10 cents for delivery.
That's a graft, because the COM
pany takes cents and the boy gets
only 4. That's the way the COM
pany fools the public. 1t has al
ready been paid for that message.


"And it was because of these
things in the Western Union and
American District Telegraph COM
panies that you struck"


Rightly speaking, we didn't
strike, not this time. The COM
panies locked us out for belonging
to the union after they forced us to
organize.


well, they was unfair like l
told you, but there was more yet.
We had a clerk that was awful
hard on the kids; he was always
punchin' 'em around and was bull
